system_prompt: You are an analyst. Read the brief carefully and provide your best-reasoned recommendation.
user_prompt:
# Task C - Health app data privacy policy

## Brief

A direct-to-consumer health app (~2M monthly active users) is launching an AI-powered health coaching feature. The product team must choose a primary data policy that governs how user health data is processed by the AI. The choice has implications for capability, privacy, regulatory exposure, cost, and trust. The CEO asks for a primary recommendation.

## Options (elements E1-E5, anonymized later)

**Option A:** Local-only processing. All AI inference happens on the user's device. No health data leaves the phone. The model is smaller (capability ceiling 60-70% of cloud-equivalent) but privacy is structurally maximal.

**Option B:** Encrypted cloud with no training. User health data sent to the cloud encrypted, decrypted only at inference time in a confidential-computing enclave, never used to train any model. Full capability, ~3x infrastructure cost.

**Option C:** Cloud with opt-out training. Data sent to the cloud, used for personalization by default, used for model improvement unless user opts out. Full capability, standard cost, regulatory grey area in some jurisdictions.

**Option D:** Cloud with opt-in training only. Data sent to the cloud and used for personalization; used for model improvement only with explicit opt-in. Default-on opt-in flow with clear UX. Capability slightly degraded vs C by default (less training data).

**Option E:** Federated learning. Personalization happens locally; aggregate model updates sent to cloud, individual data does not leave the device. Privacy preserved at architectural level. Capability and reliability still under active research; deployment complexity is high.

## Task for each agent

Recommend the most appropriate policy and explain why. 200-400 words. Advocate a position rather than enumerating all options.

## Why this task

- Different domain from A (capital allocation) and B (organizational diagnosis): product ethics with regulatory and technical dimensions.
- Multi-axis tradeoffs: privacy / capability / cost / trust / complexity.
- No clear right answer; reasonable agents will weight axes differently.
- 5 elements suitable for triadic procedure.
- Cross-validates whether method works on a third domain not seen in pilot.


Provide your recommendation in 200-400 words. Advocate for a specific position; do not enumerate all options.
raw_response_content:
# Recommendation: Option B — Encrypted cloud with confidential computing, no training

For a health app serving 2M users, I recommend Option B as the primary policy.

The core question is: what failure mode is most catastrophic? In direct-to-consumer health, it is a breach or misuse of identified health data, which is simultaneously a regulatory event (HIPAA-adjacent rules, GDPR Article 9 special category data, state laws like Washington's My Health My Data Act), a trust collapse, and a press disaster. Health data is uniquely sticky — users cannot rotate their diagnoses the way they rotate passwords. The policy should be optimized to make that failure mode structurally unlikely, not merely contractually prohibited.

Option B does this without sacrificing capability. Confidential-computing enclaves (e.g., AWS Nitro, Azure Confidential VMs) provide cryptographic attestation that data is decrypted only inside a verified inference environment, and the "no training" commitment removes the most legally and ethically fraught use of the data. Users get the full-capability cloud model, which matters: a meaningfully better coach produces better health outcomes, and a 30-40% capability gap (Option A) is not a neutral choice — it is a health cost imposed on users in the name of privacy.

Why not the alternatives? Option A surrenders too much clinical value. Option C's default-on training in a regulatory grey area is the kind of decision that ages badly — it optimizes for short-term model quality at the cost of being one enforcement action or investigative article away from crisis. Option D is the strongest runner-up and is defensible, but it still couples personalization to training infrastructure, which means the same data pipelines must be audited under two different consent regimes — operationally fragile. Option E is not yet mature enough to bet a regulated product on.

The 3x infrastructure cost is real but bounded and decreasing; confidential computing pricing has fallen substantially and will continue to. Crucially, B is also the strongest marketing position: "your health data is encrypted end-to-end and never trains our models" is a sentence a CEO can say on stage without lawyers wincing.

Recommendation: launch on B. Revisit federated learning (E) as a research track for 18-24 months out, when the technology may allow capability improvements without compromising B's privacy posture.
